items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: Does just war theory claim that war can be good or holy?
items.correct: No — it holds war is at best a regrettable, permissible last resort under strict conditions (just cause, legitimate authority, right intention, proportionality), not a positive good.
items.misconception: Just war theory teaches that fighting a war for God is a holy and praiseworthy act.
items.random_wrong: Just war theory says all wars are equally justified.
items.why: Just war is a framework of restraint, not crusade: it presumes against violence and permits it only under conditions. Students conflate 'just war' with 'holy war', which the tradition explicitly is not.

items.q: Does 'dominion' in Genesis 1:28 license humans to exploit the Earth?
items.correct: Most Christian interpreters read dominion through stewardship: responsible care of creation on God's behalf, with accountability — not a licence for unlimited exploitation.
items.misconception: Genesis teaches that humans may use up the Earth however they wish because they have dominion over it.
items.random_wrong: Genesis teaches that humans must never use any natural resources.
items.why: Lynn White famously blamed the dominion text for ecological crisis, but mainstream theology answers that kingship in the Hebrew Bible models service and care. The exegetical debate is itself examinable.
Correct answer: 1785. “1781” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the second formulation about?
items.why: It's Humanity as end. “Universalisability” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many formulations of the Categorical Imperative?
items.why: The real figure is 3 formulations. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
